title: Clinical Technologist — Medical Physics
how_to_become: You must complete the Institute of Physics and Engineering in Medicine (IPEM) Clinical Technologist Training Scheme to become a clinical technologist in medical physics.
description: Clinical technologists in medical physics work in one of three specialist areas: nuclear medicine, radiation physics, or radiotherapy physics. They focus on safe patient care and technical compliance.
specialisms: Nuclear medicine — using gamma cameras to take images of the inside of the body for diagnosis and radiopharmaceuticals to treat patients.
Radiation physics — monitoring patients, staff, and the environment for hazards from medical use of ionising and non-ionising radiation.
Radiotherapy physics — treatment planning, dose measurement, and quality control of systems used in radiotherapy treatment.

entry_requirements.overview: A qualification at SCQF level 7 or above is required to apply for the IPEM Clinical Technologist Training Scheme.
example_qualifications: HNC Applied Sciences at SCQF level 7
HND Mechanical Engineering at SCQF level 8

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to college, university, or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.training_scheme: IPEM Clinical Technologist Training Scheme at SCQF level 9, delivered in the workplace over 2 years.
education_and_training.structure: Practical skills are assessed throughout. A portfolio of work is completed to achieve the IPEM diploma.
education_and_training.registration: Once qualified, you can join the Register of Clinical Technologists (RCT).
education_and_training.alternative_routes: Some health boards in Scotland offer apprenticeship-style programmes.
education_and_training.widening_access: The Scottish Wider Access Programme (SWAP) supports adult learners with few or no qualifications to enter higher education.

learning_and_development.overview: Clinical technologists in medical physics must keep knowledge and skills up to date through CPD. The RCT audits CPD activities annually.

career_progression.overview: With experience, clinical technologists can move into training supervision or academic research.
progression_roles: Academic research with MSc or PhD

professional_bodies: Institute of Physics and Engineering in Medicine (IPEM)

chunks.text: To become a clinical technologist in medical physics, complete the IPEM Clinical Technologist Training Scheme, a 2-year work-based programme at SCQF level 9.
Clinical technologists in medical physics work in nuclear medicine, radiation physics, or radiotherapy physics, focusing on safe patient care and technical compliance.

Specialisms include nuclear medicine (gamma cameras and radiopharmaceuticals), radiation physics (monitoring radiation hazards for patients, staff, and environment), and radiotherapy physics (treatment planning, dose measurement, and quality control).

Useful school subjects include English, Maths, Physics, Science, Design and Technology, Computing Science, and Biology. A qualification at SCQF level 7 or above is required, such as HNC Applied Sciences, HND Mechanical Engineering, or BSc (Hons) Physics or Radiography.

The IPEM Clinical Technologist Training Scheme is a 2-year work-based programme at SCQF level 9, with practical skills assessment and a portfolio of work leading to the IPEM diploma. On completion, registration with the RCT is available. Some health boards offer apprenticeship-style programmes.
Clinical technologists in medical physics must maintain CPD including research, post-qualification training, and training on new equipment. The RCT audits CPD activities annually.
With experience, clinical technologists can become training supervisors or move into academic research with an MSc or PhD.
Clinical technologists in medical physics can join the Register of Clinical Technologists (RCT) and the Institute of Physics and Engineering in Medicine (IPEM).
